title: Planning Fallacy

content.statement: People consistently underestimate how long and costly tasks will be.
content.question: What is the outside-view estimate from similar past projects, and how does it compare to my inside-view plan?
content.equation: Realistic Estimate = Inside-View Estimate × Outside-View Multiplier
content.mental_model: Weather forecaster who only predicts sunshine.
content.business_lens: For estimates, average outside-view (analogous projects) and inside-view; add explicit buffers for unknowns.
content.life_lens: Assume things will take 1.5–3x your initial estimate; design for slack.
sop: Use the higher of the two estimates
Add explicit buffer for unknown unknowns
content.explainer: Major infrastructure projects routinely come in late and over budget because planners use only inside-view estimates. Studies show software projects average ~150% of estimated time. The cure is reference-class forecasting.
questions: What is the outside-view estimate from similar past projects, and how does it compare to my inside-view plan?
Look at your last three deadlines. What's the real multiplier between what you promised and what it took, and are you using that number for your next estimate?
If someone who'd run five projects just like yours gave an honest timeline, would it be longer or shorter than the one you just gave your investors?

definitions.def: Estimating based on how similar past projects actually went, rather than reasoning only from the details of this specific project.

content.equation_example: Inside-view estimate: 4 weeks. Outside-view multiplier from 5 similar past launches: 1.5x. 4 weeks times 1.5 equals a realistic estimate of 6 weeks. A higher multiplier means your past estimates have been consistently optimistic and need bigger buffers; a multiplier near 1.0 means your gut estimates are already reliable.
content.closing_statement: Your gut estimate is a starting bid, not a promise.

caption:
Principle Nº 298: Planning Fallacy

People consistently underestimate how long and costly tasks will be. Major infrastructure projects routinely come in late and over budget because planners use only inside-view estimates.

Ask yourself: What is the outside-view estimate from similar past projects, and how does it compare to my inside-view plan?
